{"id":"61bdba61-7b55-45f0-be7e-860ce7453a21","role":"user","parts":[{"type":"text","text":"<system-reminder># System Information\n\nOperating System: linux\nDefault Shell: \nHome Directory: /home/user\nCurrent Working Directory: /home/user/project\nCurrent Time: Sat Mar 07 2026 19:18:38 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)\n\n# Current Working Directory (/home/user/project) Files\n.jj\nsrc\n.gitignore\nsrc/auth.py\nsrc/notify.py\nsrc/payment.py\n\n# GIT STATUS\nCurrent branch: HEAD\nMain branch (you will usually use this for PRs): \n\nRecent commits:\nf2760fd feat: add notification service\n2a44029 feat: add payment processing\n7749c25 feat: add authentication module\n\n# TODOs\nNo TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>"},{"type":"text","text":"# CI Release Bookmark Workflow\n\n## Context\n\nYou are a build/release engineer maintaining a CI pipeline for a project stored in a `jj` (Jujutsu) repository. The repository is located at `/home/user/project`. The repo already has a short history: a root commit, then three sequential feature commits stacked on top of each other.\n\nThe three commits are described as:\n\n1. `feat: add authentication module` — introduces `src/auth.py`\n2. `feat: add payment processing` — introduces `src/payment.py`\n3. `feat: add notification service` — introduces `src/notify.py`\n\nAll three commits are currently mutable (not immutable/tagged). The working copy (`@`) sits on top of commit 3 as an empty change.\n\n## Your Assignment\n\nYou need to prepare this repository for a `v1.0` release using `jj` commands only. The release workflow requires the following steps, which you must perform **in order**:\n\n### Step 1 — Squash the three feature commits into a single release commit\n\nThe three sequential feature commits (`feat: add authentication module`, `feat: add payment processing`, `feat: add notification service`) must be combined into one commit. The final squashed commit must include all three source files (`src/auth.py`, `src/payment.py`, `src/notify.py`) and must have the description:\n\n```\nrelease: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification\n```\n\nThe working copy (`@`) should remain as an empty commit on top of this squashed commit after the squash operation.\n\n**Hint:** Think about how to squash a range of commits into one. You will need to work from the bottom of the stack upward. Using `jj log` to inspect the current state before acting is a good first step. You can refer to revisions by their description or by walking the parent chain from `@`.\n\n### Step 2 — Create a bookmark named `release/v1.0` pointing at the squashed commit\n\nOnce you have a single squashed release commit, create a bookmark named `release/v1.0` that points **at the squashed release commit** (the parent of the current working copy, i.e. `@-`).\n\nBookmarks in `jj` are created with `jj bookmark create`. Make sure the bookmark targets the correct revision.\n\n### Step 3 — Set a tag named `v1.0` on the squashed release commit\n\nIn addition to the bookmark, create a tag named `v1.0` pointing at the same squashed release commit (`@-`).\n\nTags in `jj` are managed with `jj tag set`.\n\n### Step 4 — Verify the final state\n\nAfter completing the steps above, run `jj log` and `jj bookmark list` to confirm:\n\n* There is exactly **one non-root, non-working-copy mutable commit** in the history (the squashed release commit).\n* The squashed commit has the description `release: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification`.\n* The squashed commit contains the files `src/auth.py`, `src/payment.py`, and `src/notify.py`.\n* A bookmark named `release/v1.0` exists and points to the squashed commit.\n* A tag named `v1.0` exists and points to the squashed commit.\n* The working copy (`@`) is an empty commit that is a child of the squashed commit.\n\n## Starting Environment Assumptions\n\n* Home directory: `/home/user`\n* The `jj` repository is at `/home/user/project`\n* `jj` is installed and on `$PATH`\n* `git` is installed (the repo is a jj-git-backed repo)\n* User identity is pre-configured: `user.name = \"CI Bot\"`, `user.email = \"ci@example.com\"`\n* There are exactly three feature commits stacked linearly above the root commit\n* The working copy is empty and sits on top of the third feature commit\n* No bookmarks or tags exist at the start\n\n## Important Notes\n\n* Do **not** use `git` commands directly. All operations must use `jj`.\n* Do **not** use `sudo`.\n* The bookmark name must be exactly `release/v1.0` (with a forward slash).\n* The tag name must be exactly `v1.0`.\n* The squashed commit description must be exactly: `release: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification` (note the em dash `—`).\n* All three source files must be present in the squashed commit's tree."}]}

I'm trying to decide on the best order of operations to execute the squashing process efficiently.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"pvXiONDwy2QaA3VQ","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"jj squash --help"},"output":{"output":"Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n\r\nWithout any options, moves the changes from the working-copy revision to the parent revision.\r\n\r\nWith the `-r` option, moves the changes from the specified revision to the parent revision. Fails if\r\nthere are several parent revisions (i.e., the given revision is a merge).\r\n\r\nWith the `--from` and/or `--into` options, moves changes from/to the given revisions. If either is\r\nleft out, it defaults to the working-copy commit. For example, `jj squash --into @--` moves changes\r\nfrom the working-copy commit to the grandparent.\r\n\r\nIf, after moving changes out, the source revision is empty compared to its parent(s), and\r\n`--keep-emptied` is not set, it will be abandoned. Without `--interactive` or paths, the source\r\nrevision will always be empty.\r\n\r\nIf the source was abandoned and both the source and destination had a non-empty description, you\r\nwill be asked for the combined description. If either was empty, then the other one will be used.\r\n\r\nIf a working-copy commit gets abandoned, it will be given a new, empty commit. This is true in\r\ngeneral; it is not specific to this command.\r\n\r\nThe name \"squash\" comes from the idea of combining (squashing) the changes from multiple revisions\r\ntogether.\r\n\r\nEXPERIMENTAL FEATURES\r\n\r\nAn alternative squashing UI is available via the `-o`, `-A`, and `-B` options. Using any of these\r\noptions creates a new commit. They can be used together with one or more `--from` options (if no\r\n`--from` is specified, `--from @` is assumed).\r\n\r\nUsage: jj squash [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Move only changes to these paths (instead of all paths)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into its parent (default: @). Incompatible with the experimental\r\n          `-o`/`-A`/`-B` options\r\n\r\n  -f, --from <REVSETS>\r\n          Revision(s) to squash from (default: @)\r\n\r\n  -t, --into <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into (default: @)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --to]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to use as parent for the new commit (can be repeated to\r\n          create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit after (can be repeated to create a\r\n          merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit before (can be repeated to create\r\n          a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The description to use for squashed revision (don't open editor)\r\n\r\n  -u, --use-destination-message\r\n          Use the description of the destination revision and discard the description(s) of the\r\n          source revision(s)\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to squash\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -k, --keep-emptied\r\n          The source revision will not be abandoned\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want.
